coredata.dc:description: As an advanced fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have superior potential and booming prospects. Nevertheless, the fuzzy near-infrared (NIR) fluorescence mechanism is the biggest obstacle to the sustainable development and application of CDs. Herein, N, Cl and P doped carbon dots (N,Cl,P-CDs) with NIR photoluminescence are prepared by hydrothermal. NIR photoluminescence mechanism of N,Cl,P-CDs is systemically studied. The results reveal that the protonation of aromatic amino group changes the molecular state of N,Cl,P-CDs, which plays a vital role in adjusting the energy gap between the π → π* transition via inserting multiple n orbitals, thereby making the electrons in the excited singlet state come to the lower energy level by means of vibration relaxation and narrowing the photon transition gap for emitting NIR fluorescence. Furthermore, N,Cl,P-CDs-based fluorescence method is fabricated for determination of acid phosphatase (ACP) activity relied on quenching of fluorescence by sodium hexametaphosphate (SHMP), which can be hydrolyzed by ACP to the restoration of fluorescence. The linear range for ACP activity is 0.25 to 1.25 U·L−1 with a detection limit of 0.14 U·L−1 . Through the screening of ACP inhibitors, it was found that Hg2+ could significantly reduce the hydrolysis of SHMP by ACP, forming an “off-on-off” fluorescence sensor. The feasibility of this sensor for Hg2+ was analyzed, and Hg2+ was quantified from 0.625 to 6.25 μM with a detection limit of 37.14 nM. Moreover, the “AND” and “OR” logic gates are constructed based on the interaction among SHMP, ACP and Hg2+. As greener and sustainable fluorescent nanomaterials, N,Cl,P-CDs-based fluorescence method has remarkable advantages and potential application for ACP determination, inhibitor screening and Hg2+ assay in food, medicine and environmental safety assessment.

(D) Fluorescence emission spectra, (E) FT-IR spectra and (F) zeta potential of N,Cl,P-CDs before and after NaHCO3 treatment.

(E) Proposed NIR-photoluminescence mechanism of N,Cl,P-CDs.

(F) The relationship between ΔF/F of N,Cl,P-CDs against the concentrations of ACP. The inset is the fitted curve of ΔF/F against the concentrations of ACP. ΔF represent the change of the fluorescence intensity of N,Cl,P-CDs/SHMP with and without ACP, and F is the fluorescence intensity of N,Cl,P-CDs/SHMP system.

(A) Inhibition efficiency of Na2MoO4 on N,Cl,P-CDs/SHMP/ACP. (B) The selectivity of “off–on-off” type fluorescence sensor for Hg2+ detection. The concentration of ions and small molecules is 50 μM. F and F0 represent the fluorescence emission peak intensity of N,Cl,P-CDs/SHMP/ACP with and without ions or small molecules, respectively. (n = 5). (C) The relationship between F0/F of N,Cl,P-CDs/SHMP/ACP and the concentration of Hg2+. The inset is the fitted curve of F0/F against the concentration of Hg2+. (n = 5). (D) The histograms represent the values of Hg2+ (5 μM) detection via N,Cl,P-CDs/SHMP/ACP for 30 times, and the average value of the detecting results is shown as the blue bar. F and F0 represent the fluorescence emission peak intensity of N,Cl,P-CDs/SHMP/ACP with and without Hg2+, respectively. (E) Schematic diagram of logic gate based on N,Cl,P-CDs/SHMP/ACP/Hg2+. head.ce:title: Near-infrared emissive mechanism of carbon dots induced by amino protonation and application for acid phosphatase determination, inhibitor screening and assay

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As an advanced fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have superior potential and booming prospects. Nevertheless, the fuzzy near-infrared (NIR) fluorescence mechanism is the biggest obstacle to the sustainable development and application of CDs. Herein, N, Cl and P doped carbon dots (N,Cl,P-CDs) with NIR photoluminescence are prepared by hydrothermal. NIR photoluminescence mechanism of N,Cl,P-CDs is systemically studied. The results reveal that the protonation of aromatic amino group changes the molecular state of N,Cl,P-CDs, which plays a vital role in adjusting the energy gap between the π → π* transition via inserting multiple n orbitals, thereby making the electrons in the excited singlet state come to the lower energy level by means of vibration relaxation and narrowing the photon transition gap for emitting NIR fluorescence. Furthermore, N,Cl,P-CDs-based fluorescence method is fabricated for determination of acid phosphatase (ACP) activity relied on quenching of fluorescence by sodium hexametaphosphate (SHMP), which can be hydrolyzed by ACP to the restoration of fluorescence. The linear range for ACP activity is 0.25 to 1.25 U·L−1 with a detection limit of 0.14 U·L−1 . Through the screening of ACP inhibitors, it was found that Hg2+ could significantly reduce the hydrolysis of SHMP by ACP, forming an “off-on-off” fluorescence sensor. The feasibility of this sensor for Hg2+ was analyzed, and Hg2+ was quantified from 0.625 to 6.25 μM with a detection limit of 37.14 nM. Moreover, the “AND” and “OR” logic gates are constructed based on the interaction among SHMP, ACP and Hg2+. As greener and sustainable fluorescent nanomaterials, N,Cl,P-CDs-based fluorescence method has remarkable advantages and potential application for ACP determination, inhibitor screening and Hg2+ assay in food, medicine and environmental safety assessment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Acid phosphatase (ACP) is an essential phosphomonoesterase that effectively catalyzes the hydrolysis of phosphate esters and releases free phosphate groups in a weakly acidic (optimal pH of 4.8) environment [1]. Although ACP is less abundant in mammalian cells, it plays a crucial role in regulating a variety of cellular functions and the abnormally elevated ACP level frequently indicates that some serious diseases such as hyperparathyroidism, multiple myeloma, and metastatic prostate cancer are occurring [2]. In addition, ACP features an integral role in metabolic processes as a phosphate scavenger in animals, plants, bacteria and fungi. However, ACP inhibitors will greatly hinder the above effects, lead to metabolic dysfunction of the organism, and thus affect the detection of related diseases to a certain extent. At the same time, ACP is also a key enzyme that relates to the degradation process of inosinic acid, an umami substance in many aquatic products, and utilizing the ACP inhibitors is one of the main measures to maintain the umami flavor [3]. Plus, the enzyme-inhibition- based fluorescent sensor is significantly improved in terms of portability, sensitivity, and selectivity for Hg2+ detection applications where Hg2+ is taking as an ACP inhibitor. Therefore, it is a challenge to develop a simple and sensitive sensor to quantitatively screen for ACP inhibitors.

Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted tremendous attention due to their outstanding chemical and optical stability, biocompatibility, and low toxicity since their first discovery in 2004 [4]. CDs have developed rapidly in sensors, drug delivery and bio-imaging fields recently [5], but its near-infrared (NIR) photoluminescence mechanism has not been thoroughly studied so far, which includes quantum confinement effects, molecular and surface state control, sp2 carbon domain expansion, etc [6,7]. However, these usually contained complex theoretical assumptions and difficulty to implement experimental evidence that will severely limit the purposeful regulation of the photoluminescence behavior [8,9]. Moreover, NIR has the advantages of minimum auto-fluorescence interference, low background interference, deep tissue penetration capability and high resolution, resulting in better biological detection, fluorescence imaging and disease diagnosis in living organisms with minimal damage to biological samples [10–13]. In recent years, NIR-CDs have gained tremendous attention. For example, Zhang et al. fabricated a NIR fluorescence platform by assembling of NIR-emitting CDs onto cobalt oxyhydroxide nanoflakes for ACP detection [14]. In this system, the nanoflakes induce fluorescence quenching due to energy transfer, while ACP can effectively hydrolyze ascorbic acid-2-phosphate trisodium salt to produce ascorbic acid which in turn reduces the nanoflakes, leading to fluorescence recovery. Zeng et al. prepared NIR-emissive carbon dots through a facile hydrothermal method using tetra (4-carboxyphenyl) porphyrin and thiourea as precursors [15]. The obtained CDs not only featured high selectivity and sensitivity for Hg2+, but had good biocompatibility and feasibility in bioimaging. Therefore, it is imperative to find a simple and feasible modification and synthesis method that enables CDs to exhibit NIR fluorescence behavior, and to conduct more in-depth research on the NIR fluorescence mechanism of CDs.

In this study, red-emissive nitrogen, chlorine and phosphorus doped carbon dots (N,Cl,P-CDs) with positivity and high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method using 4–chloro–o–phenylenediamine and phosphoric acid as precursors, which were characterized by TEM, fluorescence spectrum, FT-IR and XPS. The N,Cl,P-CDs have outstanding stability and red emission properties, but strong pH-dependent behavior. NIR fluorescence mechanism will be systemically analyzed. This study will focuse on the effect of amino protonation on the NIR emission of N,Cl,P-CDs, elucidate the photoluminescence mechanism, and provide an effective pathway for purposed regulating NIR fluorescence emission of CDs. Furthermore, the fluorescence sensor of N,Cl,P-CDs is fabricated for detection of acid phosphatase (ACP) and screening the ACP inhibitors based on electrostatic interaction and dynamic quenching of sodium hexametaphosphate (SHMP), which can be hydrolyzed by ACP to the restoration of fluorescence (See Scheme 1 ). Through inhibitor screening, Hg2+ is identified as a potential ACP inhibitor, because it can significantly suppresses the hydrolysis of SHMP by ACP. Thus, an “off-on-off” fluorescence sensor for quantitative detection of Hg2+ is constructed. To the best of our knowledge, no fluorescent sensor based on ACP inhibition for Hg2+ detection has been reported. So, by green synthesis of carbon dots, this N,Cl,P-CDs-based fluorescence system offers a promising approach for applications in clinical medicine and contributes to the sustainable development of environmental safety and human health.

All chemicals and reagents were acquired with analytical grade and utilized without any further purification. H3PO4, 4-chloro-o-phenylenediamine, rhodamine B, NaNO2, Na2MoO4, SHMP, NaHCO3 and dopamine were bought from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NaCl, KCl, FeCl3, MnCl2, FeSO4, HgCl2, H2O2, sodium acetate, acetic acid, glucose, D-mannose, L-arginine and L-histidine were acquired from China National Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Lactose and uric acid were purchased from Shanghai yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glutathione (GSH), adenosine triphosphate (ATP) and acid phosphatase (ACP) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Expect for ACP was dissolved in acetic acid-sodium acetate buffer, all the solutions were prepared in deionized water.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images characterized by a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 TEM of Philips FEI from the Netherlands. The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were obtained by NICOLET iS50 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of N,Cl,P-CDs was recorded with a UV-2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) analysis were performed on a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI electron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Fluorescence spectra and fluorescence lifetime were measured by Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA) and FS5 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, England), respectively. Zeta (ζ) potential was obtained from Litesizer™ 500 Nanoparticle Size and Zeta Potential Analyzer (Anton Paar, Austria).

ce:section.ce:section-title: N,Cl,P-CDs synthesis with optimization the amount of the added H3PO4 and purification
ce:section.ce:para: N,Cl,P-CDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method according to the previously reported literature with several modifications [9]. Briefly, 1.0 mmol of 4-chloro-o-phenylenediamine and 1.0 mL of concentrated phosphoric acid were dissolved in 24 mL deionized water and subsequently 5 mL of anhydrous ethanol was added for improving the precursor’s solubility. For optimization, the specific raw material ratio of N,Cl,P-CDs synthesis was shown in Table 1 . After sonication for 5 min, the homogeneous solution was obtained and transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel laboratory autoclave with a capacity of 50 mL successively. Thenceforth, the autoclave was heated to 180 °C in a hot air oven and reacted for 10 h. When the autoclave was naturally cooled down to room temperature, a cyan solution was obtained for following applications by centrifuging at 10000 rpm for 15 min and filtering the supernatant through 0.22 μm microporous membranes. Furthermore, to acquire the lyophilized N,Cl,P-CDs for further characterization, the cyan solution was dialyzed against deionized water through a dialysis membrane (molecular weight cut-off: 100 ∼ 500 Da) for 24 h to remove the small-molecule impurities. Then the purified dialysate was dried through a freeze-dryer and redispersed in deionized water, stored at 4 °C in a laboratory refrigerator. The obtained origin N,Cl,P-CDs was set as the protonation group, while the N,Cl,P-CDs with saturated NaHCO3 treatment was set as the deprotonation group.

The relative PLQY of N,Cl,P-CDs was determining by referring to rhodamine B (QY = 31 % λex = 520 nm) in water [16]. In detail, N,Cl,P-CDs and rhodamine B were diluted in water with the absorbance below 0.10 to minimize the reabsorption effects. Subsequently, the PLQY was calculated according to the following equation by combining both UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra:

N,Cl,P-CDs synthesized in different H3PO4 amounts were mounted in a fluorescence cuvette and placed in a FS5 fluorescence spectrophotometer sample pool for decay kinetics by a time-dependent single-photon counting method (TCSPC). The detection results are linearly fitted by Origin 2021 to calculate the fluorescence lifetime τave of each N,Cl,P-CDs.

To investigate the band structure and explore the mechanism of NIR fluorescence emission, UPS and UV–vis absorption spectra were analyzed according to the following formula and Tauc method, respectively.

where “EHOMO” symbolizes the energy level of highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), “Eincident photon” represents the incident energy which is 21.22 eV owing to the excitation of He 1 light source, “Ecutoff” indicates the secondary electron cutoff and “Efermi” is near Fermi energy edge.

The NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 4.8) was prepared by dropwise adding acetic acid to 10 mM sodium acetate solution for adjusting an appropriate pH value. To screen the potential ACP inhibitors, different materials including Na2MoO4 (one of the typical ACP inhibitors), common ions and small molecules were dissolved in NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 4.8) and 250 μL of them was severally added to a mixing solution which was consisted of 125 μL of SHMP (20 μM) and 125 μL of ACP (1.25 U·L−1), whereupon the mixture was taken a 7-min incubation in 37 °C and mixed with 1.5 mL of N,Cl,P-CDs for the subsequent fluorescence intensity recording and the inhibition efficiency is calculated according to the following equation.

where “Fno inhibitor” and “Finhibitor” are the fluorescence emission intensity at 680 nm of N,Cl,P-CDs/SHMP/ACP with and without Na2MoO4, respectively, and “F0” represents the origin fluorescence emission intensity of N,Cl,P-CDs/SHMP.

Different concentrations of ACP from 0 to 6.25 U·L−1 was prepared in NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 4.8), and then 250 μL of SHMP (20 μM) were mixed with the same volume of ACP solution for a 90-min lucifugal incubation in 37 °C. After that, the mixture and 1.5 mL of N,Cl,P-CDs were mingled together to measure the fluorescence emission spectra.

Different concentrations of Hg2+ from 0 to 50.0 μM was prepared in NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 4.8), and then 125 μL of ACP (1.25 U·L−1) and 125 μL of SHMP (20 μM) were added in the 250 μL of Hg2+ solution for a 7-min lucifugal incubation in 37 °C. After that, the mixture and 1.5 mL of N,Cl,P-CDs were mingled together to measure the fluorescence emission spectra.

For real samples analysis, urine from a healthy volunteer was collected as an actual biological sample for ACP activity determination. Fish blood of Ophiocephalus argus Cantor, lake water of Siyu and tap water from Fujian Medical University were selected for the measurement of Hg2+ concentration, using the standard spike method. These samples were firstly centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min, and filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove the large solid impurities, eliminate the interferences and improve the recovery. Afterwards, these samples were diluted 10 times and different concentrations of ACP or HgCl2 were added to prepare the spiked samples, and the detection conditions of the real samples were similar to these of ACP or Hg2+ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimized synthesis and characterization of N,Cl,P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The N,Cl,P-CDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method using 4-chloro-o-phenylenediamine, concentrated H3PO4 and deionized water. To prepare NIR emission CDs with high PLQY and fluorescence lifetime, we optimized the different amounts of H3PO4 during the synthesis procedure on the generation of photoluminescence N,Cl,P-CDs (Table 1). The results are shown in Fig. 1 A, the emission spectra of these five N,Cl,P-CDs is similar, with the strongest emission peak appearing at 680 nm after excitation at 614 nm. With the addition of H3PO4, the fluorescence intensity gradually increases and the inflection point is 1 mL where N,Cl,P-CDs-4 exhibit the strongest intensity. The PLQYs of the five N,Cl,P-CDs were further investigated (Fig. 1B) and combining the fluorescence lifetime of these N,Cl,P-CDs (Fig. S1), N,Cl,P-CDs-4 had the highest PLQY and longest fluorescence lifetime, with 14.38 % and 1.94 ns, respectively. Therefore, the addition amount of 1 mL H3PO4 was chosen as the final N,Cl,P-CDs synthesis protocol and used for subsequent experiments.

The morphology and size distribution of prepared N,Cl,P-CDs are characterized by TEM and high-resolution TEM image with scale bars at 50 and 5 nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. 1C and the inset. The image reveals that the prepared N,Cl,P-CDs features a monodisperse amorphous spherical particle and the particle size ranges from 2.5 to 6.5 nm with an average size of 4.51 ± 0.98 nm. In addition, the surface compositions of N,Cl,P-CDs were investigated by XPS and FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1D, the FT-IR spectrum of N,Cl,P-CDs features strong vibration at 3469 cm−1 and dispersive vibration bands range from 2000 to 3000 cm−1 involving 2881, 2779 and 2406 cm−1, attributing to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl (OH)/amino (NH2) and ammonium group (NH4 +), respectively [8]. In the meanwhile, vibration peaks located at 1657, 1413, 1194 and 1138 cm−1 are successively ascribed to the stretching vibration of carbonyl (CO), C-N, aromatic pyrrole ring and C-O [17–19]. The characteristic absorption peaks of P and Cl are revealed in 1062 cm−1 (C-P), 825 cm−1 (P-OH) and 673 cm−1 (C-Cl), which demonstrate the successful doping of P and Cl [20].

To further certify the elemental composition of N,Cl,P-CDs, XPS was carried out and there are five classic peaks observed, which centered successively at 284.8 eV (C1s), 401.6 eV (N1s), 531.4 eV (O1s), 134.0 eV (P2p) and 196.5 eV (Cl2p) in the survey spectra of N,Cl,P-CDs (Fig. 1E), indicating the N,Cl,P-CDs are mainly constitutive of C (58.69 %), N (6.36 %), O (20.72 %), P (9.59 %) and Cl (4.65 %) elements [21]. High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s in Fig. 1F are deconvoluted into four surface composition, corresponding to sp2 carbons (CC/CC, 284.8 eV), sp3 carbons (CN/CO, 285.0 eV), CP (286.0 eV) and carbonyl carbons (CO/CCl, 287.0 eV) [9,21]. As exhibited in Fig. 1G, the N1s spectra of N,Cl,P-CDs display three peaks at 399.8, 400.8 and 401.6 eV, which correspond to pyrrolic N, amino and graphitic N [22]. O1s (Fig. S2) and P2p (Fig. 1H) can be divided into two peaks, indicating the CO (531.4 eV), CO (532.4 eV), PO (133.2 eV) and PC (134.0 eV) are contained by N,Cl,P-CDs [21,23].High-resolution XPS spectra of Cl2p are shown in Fig. 1I, the chlorine valence of CCl (196.5 eV), Cl2p3/2 (197.6 eV), Cl2p (199.5 eV) and Cl2p1/2 (201.1 eV) are resolved in N,Cl,P-CDs [9]. All the XPS results conform to FT-IR characterization, and the analysis elucidates the surface of N,Cl,P-CDs is abundant in OH, NH2, NH4 +, P-OH and COOH, which result in the great water-solubility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical properties and the fluorescence stability of the N,Cl,P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis absorption spectrum are recorded by dissolving the N,Cl,P-CDs in deionized water whose pH is adjusted to 1.0 by H3PO4. As shown in Fig. 2 A, N,Cl,P-CDs feature five absorption bands located at 230, 271, 411, 567 and 617 nm (red line), and the absorption band around 230 and 271 nm are assigned to the π-π* transition of aromatic CC bonds while the 411, 567 and 617 nm absorption bands are attributed to the n-π* transition of CO or C-N groups [9]. Moreover, the excitation and emission spectra indicate that the prepared N,Cl,P-CDs possess strong emission at 680 nm with a shoulder peak at 650 nm excited by 614 nm, and the inset as well as the Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) in Fig. 2B demonstrate the N,Cl,P-CDs emit bright red fluorescence with CIE coordinates (0.719, 0.281). To further investigate the photoluminescence properties of the prepared N,Cl,P-CD, the fluorescence emission properties of at different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 2C) and a three-dimension total luminescence matrix spectrum (Fig. 2D) were investigated. With the excitation wavelength changing from 510 nm to 620 nm, the emission peak at 680 nm of the fluorescence spectrum does not change, which reflects that their excitation-independent emission behavior and the particle size is relatively uniform to a certain extent [24], conforming to the previous research.

Additionally, the fluorescence stability of N,Cl,P-CDs were examined. The influence of H2O2 and NaCl solution on the intensity of N,Cl,P-CDs were determined, and the results are shown in Fig. S3A&B, which indicate that N,Cl,P-CDs can be stable in different concentrations of H2O2 solutions and NaCl solutions ranging from 0 to 1.0 M without significant intensity quenching. Notably, according to Fig. 2E, the fluorescence intensity are almost vanished when pH reaches to 3.2 and above, therefore, the prepared N,Cl,P-CDs feature a strong pH-dependent emission behavior and this phenomenon will be further investigated in subsequent studies. Since temperature and storing time are of vital importance for a wealth of analysis, Fig. 2F and Fig. S3C&D reveal that N,Cl,P-CDs have an excellent temperature stability, and their fluorescence intensity do not decrease significantly after an hour of continuous excitation and a 9-week storage. These results demonstrate that N,Cl,P-CDs were successfully prepared, with outstanding stability and red emission properties, but strong pH-dependent behavior.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Insight into NIR emissive mechanism of N,Cl,P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
As reminded by the strong pH-dependent emission behavior, a systematic comparison between protonated N,Cl,PCDs and deprotonated N,Cl,P-CDs (de-N,Cl,P-CDs) were carried out to investigate its photoluminescence mechanism. According to the UV–vis spectra in Fig. 3 A, the prepared N,Cl,P-CDs show a multitude of absorption peaks centered at 230, 271, 411, 567 and 617 nm, while the de-N,Cl,P-CDs feature no significant absorption peak in the visible region, but retain the similar absorption behavior around the near ultraviolet region. The UV–vis absorption edges are calculated incidentally via the relationship between absorption edge and Eg as the following equation [25] and Tauc method:

where “λedge” is the absorption edge or fluorescence emission peak, “h” represents Plank’s constant and “c” equals to the speed of light propagation in a vacuum or transparent medium. The transition bandwidths and absorption edges of protonated N,Cl,P-CDs are 254 nm (4.88 eV), 301 nm (4.12 eV), 456 nm (2.71 eV), 589 nm (2.11 eV) and 640 nm (1.94 eV), and these of de-N,Cl,P-CDs are calculated as 263 nm (4.71 eV), 311 nm (3.99 eV) and 519 nm (2.39 eV). Therefore, the minimum transition energy gap (Eg,min) of protonated N,Cl,P-CDs and de-N,Cl,P-CDs are 1.94 eV and 2.39 eV, respectively.

Meanwhile, Tauc plots were processed by the following formula by combining the Tauc equation and Lambert-Beer law in Tauc method. The UV–Vis spectrum of N,Cl,P-CDs were analyzed according to this equation: hν as the horizontal coordinate, while (Ahν)1/2 as the ordinate (Fig. 3B&C), and the minimum transition energy gap of N,Cl,P-CDs before and after deprotonation is 1.93 eV and 2.29 eV, respectively, almost the same as the above results.

where A, h, υ, B, c, l and Eg represents the absorbance, Planck’s constant, light frequency, a Tauc coefficient, the concentration of sample, the width of sample and transition energy gap in eV, respectively. The m value of N,Cl,P-CDs was set as 2, which is determined by the inherent optical transition property, i.e., m = 2 value corresponds to an allowed indirect transition, while m = 1/2 for an allowed direct transition [17].

Due to the transformation of molecular state of fluorophore, deprotonation will cause the variations of absorption features and strongly affect the n → π* and π → π* transition channels [25]. Fluorescence emission spectra of N,Cl,P-CDs and de-N,Cl,P-CDs are demonstrated in Fig. 3D. The emission peaks located at 650 nm and 680 nm vanished in de-N,Cl,P-CDs, with the complete absence of fluorescence peaks. According to the Einstein's light quantum hypothesis, the photon transition band gaps of protonated N,Cl,P-CDs are 1.91 eV (650 nm) and 1.82 eV (680 nm). After the deprotonation treatment, the photon transition band gaps disappeared. These phenomena illustrate that the molecular state of fluorophore tremendously dominates the N,Cl,P-CDs fluorescence mechanism and plays an essential role in triggering the NIR emission via the protonation treatment.

Furthermore, to thoroughly reveal and understand the dominated fluorophore and detailed internal band energy structure of the N,Cl,P-CDs, the FT-IR spectra, ζ potential and HOMO energy level of N,Cl,P-CDs before and after deprotonation were analyzed and compared. As the FT-IR spectra shown in Fig. 3E, the strong stretching vibration of N,Cl,P-CDs (red line) at 3471 cm−1 is caused by the OH group. The dispersive vibration bands range from 3000-2000 cm−1 involving peaks located at 2928, 2800, and 2560 cm−1, which are attributed to the stretching vibration of NH3 + groups. Meanwhile, an asymmetric deformed vibrational absorption peak belonging to NH2 disappears at 1697 cm−1 and the absorption peaks of CO, OH, C-Cl, C-P and other characteristic functional groups after protonation still retain. The FT-IR spectra of de-N,Cl,P-CDs is revealed as blue line in Fig. 3E, where their stretching vibration of OH group is located at 3462 cm−1. In comparison with the case of N,Cl,P-CDs, the continuous stretching vibration peaks of NH3 + groups ranging between 3000–2000 cm−1 disappear, as well as the asymmetric deformation vibration at 1635 cm−1 of NH3 +, while the asymmetric deformation vibration of NH2 groups locates at 1697 cm−1 appears. As shown in Fig. 3F, the ζ potential of N,Cl,P-CDs changed from + 13.40 mV to −2.33 mV after NaHCO3 treatment, which confirms the transformation of NH3 + groups into NH2 groups. This is consistent with the results of FT-IR. It follows from the above that the prominently affected fluorophore by deprotonation is the ammonium, whose protons will be vigorously snatched away by NaHCO3.

To reveal the detailed internal energy level structure of the N,Cl,P-CDs, the HOMO energy level of N,Cl,P-CDs before and after NaHCO3 treatment are both analyzed using UPS. Subsequently, the effect of amino protonation on the band structure of N,Cl,P-CDs was investigated. Full-scale UPS spectra of N,Cl,P-CDs and de-N,Cl,P-CDs are shown in Fig. S4A&B. The Ecutoff and Efermi of protonated N,Cl,P-CDs are revealed at 16.48 eV and 1.54 eV, respectively, and the HOMO energy is calculated to be −6.28 eV, according to the following equation. However, The Ecutoff and Efermi of de-N,Cl,P-CDs are detected to be 18.24 eV and 1.94 eV (Fig. 4 C&D). Therefore, the HOMO energy of de-N,Cl,P-CDs is −4.92 eV which indicates the aromatic amino protonation will lead to the decrement of HOMO energy level. Simultaneously, the LUMO energy of N, Cl, P-CDs and de-N, Cl, P-CDs are calculated to be −4.35 eV and −2.63 eV, respectively.

where “EHOMO” symbolizes the energy level of HOMO, “Eincident photon” represents the incident energy which is 21.22 eV owing to the excitation of He 1 light source, “Ecutoff” indicates the secondary electron cutoff and “Efermi” is near Fermi energy edge, “ELUMO” and “Eg,min” are the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital and the minimum transition energy/band gap, respectively.

Therefore, to be concluded from the above analysis, it is conducive to the formation of multiple n orbitals in the π → π* energy gap by protonating the aromatic amino group of N,Cl,P-CDs, which helps the electrons in the excited singlet state to reach lower energy levels by vibration relaxation, decreasing the internal HOMO energy level, narrowing the photon transition gap, thus eventually generating NIR fluorescence emission (Fig. 4E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing principle of ACP detection based on NIR fluorescence strategy
ce:section.ce:para: Previous work had been reported that there are six negative charges in SHMP, which as a fluorescence quencher could effectively quench the fluorescence of N-CDs via the aggregation-induced quenching (AIQ) with strong electrostatic interactions [26]. Enzymatic hydrolysis of SHMP by ACP leads to its disintegration and fluorescence recovery. To investigate the feasibility of this proposed NIR fluorescence strategy for ACP detection, the fluorescence intensity change at each step was recorded by fluorescence spectra with 614 nm excitation and 680 nm emission. As displayed in Fig. 5 A, after interaction with SHMP, the fluorescence intensity of N,Cl,P-CDs is significantly reduced. Correspondingly, the fluorescence of N,Cl,P-CDs can be greatly restored with the addition of SHMP/ACP reactants. Because the ACP can hydrolysis the SHMP and restore the fluorescence of the system, an “off-on” response fluorescent sensor was constructed. Therefore, the designed fluorescent sensing system based on N,Cl,P-CDs and SHMP can be used for ACP detection based on the fluorescence recovery of N,Cl,P-CDs.

In order to elucidate the mechanism of fluorescence quenching and recovery induced by SHMP and ACP, respectively, we compared the ζ potential and the time-resolved fluorescence decay of N,Cl,P-CDs before and after the addition of SHMP or the further addition of ACP. The ζ potential of N,Cl,P-CDs, SHMP and N,Cl,P-CDs/SHMP are shown in Fig. 5B. The original ζ potential of N,Cl,P-CDs is 13.32 ± 0.42 mV, while it instantly becomes −1.61 mV after interacting with SHMP (ζ = -25.90 mV), which has high negative charge because of its six negative charges. This indicated that SHMP quenching the fluorescence of N,Cl,P-CDs may be the aggregation-induced quenching by electrostatic interaction. Moreover, the fluorescence lifetime was also analyzed and shown in Fig. 5C, where the average fluorescence lifetime of N,Cl,P-CDs is changed from 1.94 ns to 1.58 ns by the interaction of SHMP. Therefore, the fluorescence lifetime of N,Cl,P-CDs after the addition of SHMP is shortened and reveals that the fluorescence quenching of N,Cl,P-CDs by SHMP also contains a dynamic quenching process [27]. Consequently, the quenching mechanism of SHMP on N,Cl,P-CDs is induced by the combined action of electrostatic interaction and dynamic quenching. Meanwhile, as the concentration of ACP gradually increased, the average fluorescence lifetime of N,Cl,P-CDs returned to 1.93 ns. This phenomenon indicates that ACP can disassociate SHMP through enzymatic hydrolysis into phosphate ions to prevent it from quenching N,Cl,P-CDs.

To obtain the greatest analytical performance for the detection of ACP, some influencing factors including the concentration of SHMP, pH, temperature and incubation time were optimized. Primarily, the SHMP caused quenching plot and the fluorescence recovery by ACP were thoroughly investigated. Fig. 5D reveals that the F0/F increases linearly as the concentration of SHMP rises in the range from 0 to 20.0 μM. To our best knowledge, the activity of ACP is critical for SHMP hydrolysis, moreover, incubation conditions are crucial for influencing fluorescence intensity of N,Cl,P-CDs. Hence, pH, temperature and incubation time of SHMP/ACP were investigated in the subsequent experiments. As shown in Fig. 5E, the fluorescence intensity of the N,Cl,P-CDs/SHMP/ACP is gradually increased with the increase of pH from 4 to 4.8. However, in the pH range from 4.8 to 5.2, the fluorescence intensity decreased gradually, thus pH 4.8 was selected to be the optimal condition. As shown in Fig. S5A, the fluorescence intensity of the N,Cl,P-CDs/SHMP/ACP reached to the top at the optimal temperature of 37 °C. In addition, three different activities (0.50, 1.00 and 1.25 U· L−1) of ACP were used to explore the effect of incubation time on the fluorescence intensity of N,Cl,P-CDs/SHMP/ACP. As can be seen from the results (Fig. S5B), the fluorescence intensity gradually increases with the increment of incubation time and reaches the plateau phase at 90 min. Therefore, the highest concentration of SHMP (20 μM), optimal pH (4.8), temperature (37 °C) and incubation time (90 min) were carried out in the following experiments.

The fluorescence emission spectra of the proposed sensor upon the addition of different concentrations of ACP were recorded. ΔF/F is employed in the quantitative analysis of ACP, and a typical plot of the ΔF/F versus ACP activity (0.25 ∼ 6.25 U·L−1) is shown in Fig. 5F. The linear regression equation ranging from 0.25 to 1.25 U·L−1 is ΔF/F = 0.1251[ACP] + 0.7472 with a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9968, and the limit detection is evaluated to be 0.14 U·L−1 (S/N = 3). Meanwhile, the analytical performance was comparable or better to previously reported methods, as shown in Table 2 .

The standard spike method was chosen to determine ACP activity in 10 times diluted urine sample. As shown in Table S1, the average recoveries range from 93.6 % to 102.7 %, and the relative deviations (RSD) are not more than 4.0 %. These results indicate the applicability of this fluorescence sensor for the detection of ACP activity in real samples via “off–on” mode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Screening ACP inhibitor and analysis of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: Since the hydrolysis effect of ACP on SHMP will be affected by ACP inhibitors, which can be screened by this proposed sensor. First, Na2MoO4, a typical ACP inhibitor, was employed as a model inhibitor to demonstrate the ability of this sensor to screen for ACP inhibitors. As shown in Fig. 6 A, with the increment of Na2MoO4, the inhibition efficiency gradually increases, and the IC50 value is calculated to be 0.719 ± 0.016 μM by logistic function fitting. The results show that the hydrolysis of SHMP by ACP can be affected in the presence of the corresponding ACP inhibitors, so this method can be effectively utilized for the screening of potential ACP inhibitors. For exploring its capability to screen potential ACP inhibitors, a plenty of substances were evaluated, including positively charges ions (K+, Na+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Hg2+), some common amino acid (L-Arg, L-His), and monosaccharide (Glucose, D-Mannose, Lactose), as well as other molecules like UA, DA, ATP, under the same experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 6B, the amino acid, monosaccharide and UA, DA, ATP had relatively weak inhibitory effects on ACP activity. Cations with high valence state, for example, Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+ and Hg2+ have different degrees of inhibitory effects on ACP activity, among which Hg2+ showed significant inhibition particularly. Therefore, Hg2+ is a potential ACP inhibitor, which could significantly reduce the hydrolysis of ACP on SHMP, forming an “off-on-off” fluorescence sensor. The screening results also show to some extent that the sensing system has good specificity for detecting Hg2+. Because Hg2+ even at low concentration can bioaccumulate in the food chain and result in damage to the kidneys and brain of humans [40], the “off-on-off” sensor will subsequently be used for Hg2+ detection.

To investigate the feasibility of this sensing system for Hg2+ assay, the fluorescence intensity change was carried out by fluorescence spectra at 680 nm after excitation at 614 nm. Under optimized experimental conditions, this sensor verified the feasibility for the determination of Hg2+. Fig. 6C exhibits that the fluorescence intensity decreases gradually with the increase of Hg2+, and a good linear regression curve can be fitted with F0/F ordinate and Hg2+ concentration (0.625 ∼ 6.25 μM). Herein, the resulting linear regression equation are F0/F = 0.1777[Hg2+] + 0.9487 with a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9940, and the limit detection is evaluated to be 37.14 nM (3σ/slope). Besides, compared with other detection methods reported in the past three years (Table 3 ), this method has similar or even better detection performance and is capable to achieve more rapid and sensitive detection of Hg2+. In addition, to ensure the reproducibility and stability of the method during detection, thirty Hg2+ standard solutions with a concentration of 5 μM were measured with this “off-on-off” fluorescence sensor at three different time nodes. The final thirty detection results are shown in Fig. 6D, with an RSD% of 1.29 %, indicating that the method has excellent reproducibility and stability. Notably, the most important component of “off-on-off” type fluorescence sensor, N,Cl,P-CDs possess various superiorities, like convenience, low cost, and great stability.

In order to prove the feasibility of the “off-on-off” fluorescence sensor in real sample assay, different spiked concentrations of Hg2+ in lake water, tap water and fish blood samples were measured. After fluorescence determination, the fluorescence emission intensity values at 680 nm were brought into the fitted linear equation and the corresponding Hg2+ concentration, recovery, and RSD were calculated. The detection results in Table S2 reveal that the spiked recovery is in the range of 96.9 %∼103.0 %, and the RSD is not higher than 3 %, indicating this method establishes reliable and accurate measurement outcome, as well as the excellent application potential in actual sample detection.

Due to the multi-target characteristic of N,Cl,P-CDs, a three-input logic gate based on the interaction among SHMP, ACP and Hg2+ or potential ACP inhibitors was constructed, shown in Fig. 6E. With the fluorescent N,Cl,P-CDs as the system, the NOT gate with SHMP as input and the AND gate with ACP and Hg2+, in which Hg2+ is combined with NOT gate, were utilized as input combination to fabricate the OR gate. In binary logic operations, the presence and absence of input were marked as “1″ and “0”, respectively. Output “1” was defined as the resumed fluorescence or high fluorescence, while output “0” symbolized the quenched fluorescence. Therefore, the output is “0” when the three inputs are imported as “1” simultaneously, conforming to the quenched fluorescence. The detailed truth value is expressed in Table S3. The construction of logic gates will potentially make contributions to food, pharmaceutical and environmental safety assessments in the future.

To sum up, a water-soluble “off–on-off” fluorescence sensor, N,Cl,P-CDs/ SHMP/ACP was successfully prepared through simple hydrothermal synthesis and fabrication. The N,Cl,P-CDs feature not only the high PLQY, but the excellent optical stability and sensitively accurate analysis towards ACP and Hg2+ detection in real samples, based on their specific response. Furthermore, N,Cl,P-CDs have strong pH-dependent emission behavior, revealing the NIR fluorescence is triggered by the aromatic amino protonation which inserts several n orbitals into the π → π* transition channel and narrows the photon transition energy gap according to the systematic analysis. The inserted n orbitals help the electron in the excited singlet state come to the lower energy level by means of vibration relaxation, and consequently emits the NIR fluorescence. Finally, based on the interaction among SHMP, ACP and Hg2+ (an ACP inhibitor), the reasonable designs of “AND” and “OR” logic gates are expected to provide support for assessing the food, pharmaceutical and environmental safety and screening for potential ACP inhibitors in the future. It is expected that this N,Cl,P-CDs-based fluorescence system can contribute to the sustainable development of environmental safety and human health.
